---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма / каротаж)** — a composite geophysical well log from a Soviet-era exploration borehole. It is a tall, narrow, multi-column document typical of Soviet-era drilling and geophysical logging practice, combining lithological description columns on the left with geophysical curve traces in the centre and analytical/interpretive data on the right.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure appears to cover a **single deep borehole** (скважина). The title at the top includes the word **"КАРОТАЖ"** (karotazh = well logging/borehole geophysics), and what appears to be a borehole or well identifier, though the specific number is **[illegible]** at this scan resolution. Based on the report context (Smolianova et al., 1983–1984, northern Kazakhstan, sheets M-42/N-42), this is likely one of the regional survey boreholes drilled to assess subsurface structure.

The **depth range** appears to extend from near-surface down to what appears to be several hundred metres (likely **0–600+ metres** based on the vertical extent of the log), though the exact scale graduations are **[illegible]** at this resolution.

---

## Key Content

The log is structured in multiple vertical columns from left to right:

### Left Side — Lithological/Drilling Columns:
- **Leftmost columns** appear to contain: borehole interval numbers, depth markers, core recovery data, and a **lithological column** (graphic log) showing rock type changes with depth using standard Soviet hatching/pattern symbols. Visible patterns suggest alternating sequences of:
  - **Shaded/dense hatching** — likely representing dense crystalline or clay-rich rocks
  - **Dotted patterns** — possibly sandstone or sandy formations
  - **Brick-like patterns** — possibly carbonates or consolidated sediments
  - **Blank/white intervals** — possibly loose sediments or zones of interest

### Centre — Geophysical Curves:
This is the dominant visual feature. **Two or more geophysical curve traces** run the full length of the log:
- A **left curve** (wider amplitude variations, sometimes deflecting strongly to the left) — likely **КС (кажущееся сопротивление / apparent resistivity)** or **ПС (потенциал самополяризации / spontaneous potential/SP)**
- A **right/central curve** (more tightly varying) — likely a **gamma-ray (ГК)** or **neutron log (НГК)** or second resistivity curve
- Several notable **anomalous zones** are visible where the curves deflect strongly, particularly:
  - A prominent **sharp peak/deflection** approximately 1/4 of the way down the log — possibly corresponding to a resistive bed (carbonate, dense rock, or mineralised zone)
  - Multiple **high-amplitude oscillations** in the middle section of the log suggesting lithological heterogeneity
  - A **relatively quiet/smooth zone** in the lower portion suggesting more homogeneous lithology

### Right Side — Analytical/Interpretive Columns:
- Several columns to the right of the curves appear to contain:
  - **Rock description text** (lithological descriptions in Russian — **[illegible]** at this resolution)
  - **Core sample analysis results** — possibly chemical assay data or porosity/permeability values in tabular form
  - **Stratigraphic/formation assignments** in narrow columns
  - **Numerical data columns** at the far right, likely containing specific geophysical values, depths of boundaries, or analytical results

## Research Value

This composite borehole log provides a continuous record of **subsurface lithology and geophysical properties** for one of the regional survey boreholes in northern Kazakhstan, allowing correlation of rock types with geophysical responses (resistivity, SP, possibly gamma-ray) across the drilled section. A geologist or AI research assistant would use this to **identify formation boundaries, anomalous zones potentially indicative of mineralisation or fluid-bearing horizons, and to calibrate geophysical survey interpretations** against ground-truth lithological data. In the context of Smolianova et al. (1983–1984), this log directly answers questions about **subsurface structure and mineral potential** by linking surface geophysical anomalies (seismic, gravity, magnetic) to the actual rock sequence encountered in drilling.
